OpenSSL Toolkit at version 1.1.1v have these vulernabilities - CVE-2024-5535, CVE-2024-2511, CVE-2023-4807, CVE-2023-5678, CVE-2024-9143, CVE-2024-4741, CVE-2024-0727, CVE-2024-13176, CVE-2025-9230

To fix these, we would like to move to 1.1.1zd, but we found that this version not officially maintained by the OpenSSL project. For the 1.1.1 series, the last officially supported release is v1.1.1w is what we learned.

Given this, we need to decide whether to upgrade to v1.1.1w or proceed with a migration to OpenSSL v3.5.5, which is the latest officially supported Long-Term Support (LTS) version. We also noticed that the official website of OpenSSL mentions that “All versions below v3.0.19 are out of support and should not be used.”

Please note that if we choose to migrate to v3.5.5, the changes will introduce breaking impacts for our product and will require significant modifications across the system.

Looking forward to recommendations on v1.1.1.zd vs 1.1.1w vs 3.5.5. What are the implications of using v1.1.1zd?

Essentially wanting export access to the individual PKEY objects inside a hybrid key. ### Discussed in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/discussions/27684
Originally posted by **RodriM11** May 22, 2025 Hi! OQSProvider currently supports the ability to, from the `EVP_PKEY *` object that holds a hybrid PQ key pair (or public key), to access both the classical and post-quantum keys (public and/or private) independently. I was wondering whether this "feature" could be extended to OpenSSL itself (I have not seen that functionality in the current release, but maybe I am wrong). While it could be (almost) straightforward to separate keys from the hybrid value (since the PQ keys have fixed lengths, and the concatenation order can be inferred from each algorithm), I think it would be better (and cleaner) for OpenSSL to provide such functionality.

Description

In TLS scenarios, the server sends a certificate request message to the client, carrying the RSA signature algorithm and certtype. When setting the certificate, the client sets the RSA certificate first and then the ECDSA certificate. The client sends an empty certificate when sending the certificate message. According to lines 3259 to 3263 in the code, the certificate sent by the client can only be the current certificate, while the server selects the appropriate certificate from all the certificates supported by the local end based on the signature algorithm.

Question

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/1f23e4102971d1986dec66e5eebffd001e71e3f5/crypto/evp/evp_pbe.c#L141-L154

In this and some more places (see https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/29921#discussion_r2769320334) we try to fetch EVP_MD object using EVP_MD_fetch and then fallback to EVP_get_digestbynid which is a wrapper around EVP_get_digestbyname.

It seems redundant to me because EVP_get_digestbyname is currently (after removing the engines) a wrapper for fetching an object from the default libctx. This fallback may cause fetching an undesired digest if we are currently in the non-default libctx, and is useless otherwise.

If I'm correct, these fallbacks should be eliminated from the library code.

items.body:
Unlike `SSL_write` and `SSL_read` that go through BIO methods, the `SSL_sendfile` function directly calls `sendfile(2)`.

This is fine when we expect to block the current thread, or poll over non-blocking sockets, but this doesn't work with a custom BIO that delegates to an internal event-loop or reactor, or use io_uring and want to fully offload sendfile to the kernel (using `IORING_OP_SPLICE`).

I don't know enough of OpenSSL internals to make a correct proposal. My naive understanding would be to introduce a `BIO_sendfile` method, for example (taking into account #29742):

```cc
int BIO_sendfile(BIO* b,
    int s, int fd, off_t off, size_t size, ssize_t *sbytes, int flags));

int BIO_meth_set_sendfile(BIO_METHOD *biom,
    int (*sendfile)(BIO *, int, int, off_t, size_t, ssize_t*, int));
```

The internal bss_sock and bss_conn implementations would take care of retry flags and to call `ktls_sendfile` instead of `SSL_sendfile`.
items.title: SSL_sendfile bypasses BIO and always calls sendfile

OpenSSL version: 3.6.1

Running `pkgconf --validate` on any of the pkgconfig files emits the following warning:
```
% pkg-config --validate /usr/lib64/pkgconfig/openssl.pc
/usr/lib64/pkgconfig/openssl.pc:4: warning: trailing whitespace encountered while parsing value section
/usr/lib64/pkgconfig/libssl.pc:4: warning: trailing whitespace encountered while parsing value section
/usr/lib64/pkgconfig/libcrypto.pc:4: warning: trailing whitespace encountered while parsing value section
```
This appears to be caused by this expression in the exporter which tries to combine a list of values into a space-separated list. This always leaves a trailing space.

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/openssl-3.6.1/exporters/pkg-config/libcrypto.pc.in#L12

I don't see how having a space-separated list in includedir would be useful anyway; under what circumstance would there be multiple values here, and how would a pkgconfig consumer be expected to use that?

If you need any more info, I am happy to provide it.

items.body:
I am developing a hardened container image for **FIPS 140-3 compliance** using OpenSSL 3.5.5 on Wolfi OS. 
According to **NIST SP 800-131A Revision 2** and **FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance**, the minimum security strength for HMAC generation must be **112 bits**. Therefore, any HMAC operation with a key shorter than 14 bytes (112 bits) must be rejected by a FIPS-validated module.

#### **The Issue**
While the FIPS provider successfully enforces other retirement policies (e.g., Triple-DES encryption is correctly blocked), it **fails to enforce the HMAC minimum key length restriction**, even when `hmac-key-check = 1` is explicitly set in the configuration.

---

#### **Reproduction Steps**

**1. Build Environment:**
The binaries are built from source using the following configuration:
```bash
./Configure enable-fips shared --prefix=/usr/local --openssldir=/usr/local/ssl
make -j$(nproc) && make install_sw install_ssldirs
```

**2. FIPS Module Installation:**
```bash
openssl fipsinstall -module /usr/local/lib/ossl-modules/fips.so -out /usr/local/ssl/fipsmodule.cnf -self_test_onload
```

**3. Configuration (`openssl.cnf`):**
I am using a layered configuration to ensure strict enforcement:
```ini
openssl_conf = openssl_init

[openssl_init]
providers = provider_sect
alg_section = algorithm_sect

[algorithm_sect]
default_properties = fips=yes

[provider_sect]
fips = fips_sect
base = base_sect

[fips_sect]
.include /usr/local/ssl/fipsmodule.cnf
activate = 1
security-checks = 1
hmac-key-check = 1
tdes-encrypt-disabled = 1
```

**4. The Verification Test:**
I tested two different FIPS policies to check for consistency:

*   **Test A: Triple-DES Retirement (Expected: FAIL, Actual: FAIL - SUCCESSFUL ENFORCEMENT)**
    ```bash
    echo "data" | openssl enc -des-ede3-cbc -e -k pass -pbkdf2
    # Result: Error (Correct, as TDES encryption is retired in FIPS mode)
    ```

*   **Test B: HMAC Key Strength (Expected: FAIL, Actual: PASS - COMPLIANCE FAILURE)**
    ```bash
    openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "1234" /dev/null
    # Result: HMAC-SHA2-256(/dev/null)= 36acf017...
    # Compliance Issue: Accepted a 32-bit key, ignoring the 112-bit requirement.
    ```

---

#### **Diagnostic Information**

*   **Provider Status:**
    `openssl list -providers` confirms `fips` provider (v3.1.2) is **active** and used.
*   **Algorithm Fetching:**
    `openssl list -cipher-algorithms -propquery fips=yes` shows algorithms correctly tagged with `@ fips`.
*   **Property Query Attempt:**
    Attempting to force enforcement via `default_properties = "fips=yes,fips.security_checks=1"` results in a startup error:
    `error:1B80006E:lib(55):ossl_parse_query:trailing characters...HERE-->-checks=1`
    It appears the property parser does not accept the hyphen in the property name, even though it is a valid provider parameter.

---

#### **Expected Behavior**
When `hmac-key-check = 1` is set in the `fips_sect`, the `EVP_MAC_init` (or underlying provider implementation) should validate the key length and return an error (e.g., `EVP_R_KEY_TOO_SMALL`) if the key length is less than 112 bits.

#### **Actual Behavior**
The FIPS provider initializes the HMAC context and performs the operation regardless of the key length, bypassing the FIPS 140-3 run-time requirement.

#### **Environment**
- **OpenSSL Version:** 3.5.5
- **FIPS Provider Version:** 3.1.2
- **OS:** Wolfi (Chainguard) - Linux 6.x
- **Architecture:** x86_64 / aarch64

---

### **Additional Context**
This issue seems to indicate that either:
1. The `hmac-key-check` parameter in `fips_config(5)` is not being propagated correctly to the MAC implementation in the 3.5.x branch.
2. The `dgst` utility (and potentially others) is not initializing the MAC context in a way that triggers this specific run-time check.

Given that FIPS 140-3 is now the standard, this lack of enforcement creates a significant gap in automated compliance validation.

Dear Maintainer,

Our team recently developed a null-pointer-dereference (NPD) vulnerability detection tool, and we used it to scan the OpenSSL (version 3.4.0). After manual review, we identified a potentially vulnerable code snippet that could lead to a null-pointer dereference bug. We would appreciate your expert insight to confirm whether this vulnerability could indeed pose a risk to the system.

In our internal CI we use the following command to test SSKDF:

`# openssl kdf -provider fips -keylen 2000 -kdfopt mac:KMAC-128 -kdfopt maclen:200 -kdfopt hexkey:b74a149a161545 -kdfopt hexinfo:348a37a2 -kdfopt hexsalt:3638271ccd68a2 SSKDF`

This used to work fine, but starts to fail since commit https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/40240719507cfc09870f0faa28cc6e70084d3ee7 "Switch FIPS self tests to deferred execution" when run under FIPS:
```
KDF parameter error
000003FFF7FC60C0:error:1C800069:Provider routines:fips_sskdf_key_check_passed:invalid key length:providers/implementations/kdfs/sskdf.c:442:
```

The error comes from within `fips_sskdf_key_check_passed()` where `ossl_kdf_check_key_size()` checks if the key size is large enough, i.e. at least 112 bits. This is of course not the case with the 7 bytes key supplied by above command. So the failure is certainly intended and works as designed.

I just wonder why this did not show up before commit https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/40240719507cfc09870f0faa28cc6e70084d3ee7 "Switch FIPS self tests to deferred execution"? I don't see that this commit changes anything at the checking, so I wonder why it only now starts to detect the too short key? Was this a bug before and is now fixed by magic ? 

@simo5

items.title: constify X509 api

items.body:
#### Background

In containerized environments, applications often cannot rely on the host’s default core-dump configuration. Core dump behavior is controlled by host-level limits and kernel settings, which may be restricted by the container runtime. As a result, some applications install fatal signal handlers and generate crash artifacts (e.g. core dumps or structured crash reports) in **userspace**.

When a process receives a fatal signal (e.g. `SIGSEGV`, `SIGABRT`) and handles it via `sigaction`, applications may need to ensure that private key material is not recoverable from those crash artifacts.

Calling `EVP_PKEY_free()` from a signal handler is not safe, as it may eventually call `free()`, which is not async-signal-safe and can result in undefined behavior. Currently, there is no supported way to reliably wipe the contents of an `EVP_PKEY` in a signal-safe context.

#### Proposal

Introduce an async-signal-safe API to clear sensitive key material without freeing memory, e.g.:

```c
int EVP_PKEY_clear(EVP_PKEY *pkey);
```

Requirements:

* Zero all sensitive key material associated with the `EVP_PKEY`
* Must not call `free()` or other non–async-signal-safe functions
* Must not allocate memory or take locks
* Safe to call from a fatal signal handler
* Leave the object allocated but in an cleared/inert state

The goal is zeroization only, not object destruction.

#### Rationale

* Reduces risk of private key exposure via core dumps
* Enables safer crash handling in security-sensitive applications
* Avoids undefined behavior/crash from calling non–signal-safe APIs
* `MADV_DONTDUMP` or similar mechanisms cannot be used

We would appreciate feedback on whether such an API would be acceptable, or whether there are alternative approaches that better align with OpenSSL’s design.
items.title: Async-signal-safe API to clear `EVP_PKEY` contents

items.body:
Postfix on Ubuntu wouldn't start. It turned out to be caused  by a totally incorrect file with `.pem` extension in `/etc/ssl/certs` that contained a `EC PRIVATE KEY`. Shouldn't be there in the 1st place. But this in turn makes `openssl rehash /path/to/certs` fail silently and exit with error level 1. And that broke Postfix' startup helper script.

It would have saved me quite some time debugging this if `openssl rehash` would log something about why it `exit(1)`'d, or it'd just log a warning and ignored the file like when it does when a `.pem`-file doesn't contain a certificate or CRL

Examples:

This is fine:
```
$ mkdir -p test
$ cd test                      
$ cp /etc/ssl/certs/*pem .                            
$ openssl rehash . ; echo $?
0
```

This is fine too, a warning was logged, exit level is 0:
```
$ date > broken.pem                 
$ openssl rehash . ; echo $?
rehash: warning: skipping broken.pem, it does not contain exactly one certificate or CRL
0
```

This is not fine. I would expect an error message here. Or a warning and error level 0.
```
$ openssl ecparam -name prime256v1 -genkey -noout -out broken.pem
$ openssl rehash . ; echo $?                                     
1
```

Consider this `dummy` provider (stolen from `<openssl>/test`).

It defines Encoders and Decoders that are annotated with property strings like `provider=dummy` and additionally `x.test1='FOO'` or `x.test2='bar'`.

On the CLI, we can often use `-propquery` to select algorithms.
The following examples are using `openssl list -verbose -all-algorithms` and variations of `-propquery "<PROPQUERYSTRING>"`.

https://docs.openssl.org/3.6/man7/property/ provides EBNF syntax for the property values, allowing options for unquoted, `'`-quoted, and `"`-quoted strings.

A reasonable expectation is that unquoted strings match as equal against their equivalent quoted representation (e.g., no difference between the value `FOO` (a valid unquoted string value) and `'FOO'` (a valid `'`-quoted string value around the same `FOO` unquoted value)).

**Indeed this equivalence seems to be true for lowercase string values, but appears to fail for uppercase string values.**

When server sends certificate request, if connection is TLSv1.3 at this code:

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/79c098525546ba5011e65f320ae2c8d1b9fd2aac/ssl/statem/statem_clnt.c#L2981-L2984

certificate types cleared. if certificates uses ec like secp256r1 etc,

https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/79c098525546ba5011e65f320ae2c8d1b9fd2aac/ssl/t1_lib.c#L4262-L4267

the for loop cannot run. because ctypes cleared at processing certificate request. and `rv` will not have `CERT_PKEY_CERT_TYPE` flag. then `tls1_check_chain` returns `0` which prevents sending client certificate.

this is a high level container to track various bits in changing the internals of how X509 keeps track of things. 

Today, X509 maintains a separate view of cached values after a TBS certificate is parsed, (and in the case of extensions even more so with cached extensions) 

Now that most of the accessors in question are const, and with an opaque ASN1_STRING, we can move to 
a model where we parse the Certificate once, and return pointers to one parsed value instead of making copies / having an internal "cache".

items.body:
Hello,

I ran into an issue recently where TLS handshakes would randomly fail on the server side with the following error repeated twice: `search only supported for directories`. I tracked it down to the `OSSL_STORE_find` call in `crypto/x509/by_store.c`. These errors, according to the discussion in [this issue](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/issues/27461), can be ignored and simply mean the store URI is a file. 

However, these errors would cause the handshake to fail. `SSL_accept` would return -1 because the socket is non-blocking and it needed a read. Then because there are errors on the error stack, `SSL_get_error` reports `SSL_ERROR_SSL` instead of `SSL_ERROR_WANT_READ`, so it appears like an unrecoverable error has occurred.

I see this has been resolved in [this PR](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/27498), but the changes there are only in 3.6 right now. It's been a while since that was merged, so are there any plans to backport it to earlier releases? I'm a little unsure of the usual process and how that is decided.

items.body:
Detecting plaintext HTTP over a TLS socket is broken for certain verbs

## Observed Behavior

Setup an `openssl s_server` instance and send a plaintext HTTP POST request to the created socket. Observe that OpenSSL does not detect that the packet was a HTTP request and instead responds with a fatal record_overflow alert. Contrast the POST request behavior to the behavior in OpenSSL version 3.1.8. For an example of this affecting end-user software, install Apache HTTPD 2.4, configure it to use TLS, and send a plaintext HTTP POST request to the Apache server port.

```
$ docker pull debian:latest
$ docker run --rm -it --pull never debian:latest /bin/bash
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# apt update
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# apt install openssl curl netcat-openbsd bsdextrautils
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# openssl version
OpenSSL 3.5.4 30 Sep 2025 (Library: OpenSSL 3.5.4 30 Sep 2025)
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# openssl ecparam -outform pem -genkey -out server.key -name prime256v1
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# openssl req -x509 -days 365 -key server.key -noenc -outform pem -out server.pem
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# openssl s_server -port 8699 -cert server.pem -key server.key
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# curl -X POST http://localhost:8699
curl: (1) Received HTTP/0.9 when not allowed
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# printf 'POST / HTTP/1.1' | nc -v localhost 8699 | hexdump -C
Connection to localhost (::1) 8699 port [tcp/*] succeeded!
00000000  15 03 03 00 02 02 16                              |.......|
00000007
```

In contrast to the previous behavior, when a GET request is sent, OpenSSL detects the plaintext GET request and closes the connection without sending any bytes.

```
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# curl http://localhost:8699
curl: (52) Empty reply from server
root@a3c4c8e748f6:/# printf 'GET / HTTP/1.1' | nc -v localhost 8699 | hexdump -C
Connection to localhost (::1) 8699 port [tcp/*] succeeded!
```

If 3.1.8 is built from source the behavior is different. I believe this is the behavior that the OpenSSL library intends to have.

```
root@63351f089e1c:/# curl -LO https://github.com/openssl/openssl/releases/download/openssl-3.1.8/openssl-3.1.8.tar.gz
<build from source>
root@9ebca902f229:/build# openssl version
OpenSSL 3.1.8 11 Feb 2025 (Library: OpenSSL 3.1.8 11 Feb 2025)
root@63351f089e1c:/openssl-3.1.8# openssl s_server -port 8699 -cert server.pem -key server.key
root@9ebca902f229:/# printf 'POST / HTTP/1.1' | nc -v localhost 8699 | hexdump -C
Connection to localhost (::1) 8699 port [tcp/*] succeeded!
root@9ebca902f229:/# printf 'GET / HTTP/1.1' | nc -v localhost 8699 | hexdump -C
Connection to localhost (::1) 8699 port [tcp/*] succeeded!
```

I tested this issue and found that this issue reproduces on Debian 13.3 and FreeBSD 15.0-current. This issue appears to have been introduced by commit [1853d20a00
](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/1853d20a008a85d327f4faa9e07be40a85549f8e). This commit appears to have been first released in OpenSSL version 3.2. Code inspection confirms that this issue should still exist in the OpenSSL master branch.

## Code Inspection

In OpenSSL 3.1.8 the bytes the client sent are processed by [ssl3_get_record](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/77e6daafbad639584e1e4877172b3f9a777966dd/ssl/record/ssl3_record.c#L173). Since a plaintext HTTP request does not match the format required for a `V2ClientHello`, the [else block on line 281](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/77e6daafbad639584e1e4877172b3f9a777966dd/ssl/record/ssl3_record.c#L281) will be executed. Between [lines 336 and 362](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/77e6daafbad639584e1e4877172b3f9a777966dd/ssl/record/ssl3_record.c#L336-L362), the `SSL3_VERSION_MAJOR` is checked to ensure that appears valid. As part of this check, the plaintext data sent by the client is checked to see if it appears to be any commonly used HTTP verbs. If the bytes the client sent don't appear to have a valid version, the code sends the appropriate response to the client. The [if statement on line 382](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/77e6daafbad639584e1e4877172b3f9a777966dd/ssl/record/ssl3_record.c#L382-L388) verifies that the TLS packet length isn't too long.

The code in the OpenSSL master branch appears to be validating the length of the `TLSPlaintext` record that the client sent before validating if the `type` or `version` fields are valid. In [tls_get_more_records](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c#L543C5-L543C25), the code has determined the bytes it received from the client is not a `V2ClientHello`. Therefore, the [body of the else statement on line 629 is executed](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c#L629-L664) to extract the first 5 bytes from the data the client sent. When the [length check on line 659](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c#L659) executes, the type is "P", the `rec_version` "OS", and length is "T " from the "POST / HTTP/1.1" request that the client sent. This check assumes that the TLS major version is set correctly and responds to the client accordingly. The validation of the record layer header is [performed on line 666](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c#L666) in the call to the validate_record_header virtual function.

The [tls_validate_record_header](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101/ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c#L44) function validates the `SSL3_VERSION_MAJOR` and also performs the plaintext HTTP checks. I believe this function needs to be called before performing the fragment length check so that the code can validate that the client has sent something that is a valid TLS header.

I believe this is a potential patch that addresses the issue, while preserving the check that the record length is greater than the number of bytes that the client sent. However, due to the [check on line 115](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101/ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c#L115-L123) it may be possible that this check can safely be removed in entirety. This patch should apply cleanly to [commit c53d784272
](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/c53d7842726d0103696e03d79abd76539c65b101).

```diff
diff --git a/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c b/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c
index aa43dba6a4..e02875366b 100644
--- a/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c
+++ b/ssl/record/methods/tls_common.c
@@ -655,12 +655,6 @@ int tls_get_more_records(OSSL_RECORD_LAYER *rl)

                 if (rl->msg_callback != NULL)
                     rl->msg_callback(0, version, SSL3_RT_HEADER, p, 5, rl->cbarg);
-
-                if (thisrr->length > TLS_BUFFER_get_len(rbuf) - SSL3_RT_HEADER_LENGTH) {
-                    RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_RECORD_OVERFLOW,
-                        SSL_R_PACKET_LENGTH_TOO_LONG);
-                    return OSSL_RECORD_RETURN_FATAL;
-                }
             }

             if (!rl->funcs->validate_record_header(rl, thisrr)) {
diff --git a/ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c b/ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c
index 9961725439..05562f7187 100644
--- a/ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c
+++ b/ssl/record/methods/tlsany_meth.c
@@ -111,6 +111,11 @@ static int tls_validate_record_header(OSSL_RECORD_LAYER *rl, TLS_RL_RECORD *rec)
                 SSL_R_WRONG_VERSION_NUMBER);
             return 0;
         }
+        if (rec->length > TLS_BUFFER_get_len(&rl->rbuf) - SSL3_RT_HEADER_LENGTH) {
+            RLAYERfatal(rl, SSL_AD_RECORD_OVERFLOW,
+                SSL_R_PACKET_LENGTH_TOO_LONG);
+            return 0;
+        }
     }
     if (rec->length > SSL3_RT_MAX_PLAIN_LENGTH) {
         /*
```

RFC 9579 technically allows to use arbitrarily long HMAC keys despite the recommendation that the key length should be equal to the output of the used hash function. OpenSSL assumes that the maximum key length is 64 bytes because no commonly used hash function has a longer output. As a result, it fails to process valid PKCS#12 certificates using such long keys.

This is an example of one such certificate (using a 256-byte HMAC key and the password "foobar") in Base64:

**Description:**
When using `curl` with a client certificate that requires a pass phrase inside an `expect` script, the password prompt (`Enter PEM pass phrase:`) sometimes visibly echoes the typed characters (i.e., the pass phrase appears in plain text). In contrast, during a normal interactive terminal session, the input should be hidden (no echo). This issue occurs intermittently.

**Environment:**

OpenSSL version: OpenSSL 1.1.1wa  16 Nov 2023

**Steps to Reproduce:**

1. Prepare a client certificate in PEM format that is protected by a pass phrase.

2. Create a shell script that uses `expect` to invoke `curl` repeatedly with the `--cert` option.
   Example :

   ```
   passwd="$1"
   cert_dir="$2"
   url="$3"
   
   while true ;do
   expect <<END 
   spawn curl -k --cacert ${cert_dir}/xxx.cer --key ${cert_dir}/xxx.pem --cert ${cert_dir}/xxx.cer -X GET https://${url}
       expect {
           "Enter PEM pass*" {
               send -- "${passwd}\r"
               exp_continue
           }
       }
   END
   sleep 1
   done
   ```

3. Run the script until the pass phrase is echoed unintentionally.

4. Observe that occasionally the entered pass phrase  is displayed in plain text on the terminal during the prompt.

**Expected Behavior:**
The pass phrase should never be echoed, regardless of how `curl` is invoked.

**Actual Behavior:**
Intermittently, the typed pass phrase is displayed in plain text, potentially exposing sensitive information in logs, terminal scrollback, or screen captures.

stack dump: 

```
#0 0xf7c12c46 in gf_sub ()
#1 0xf7c0eafa in niels_to_pt ()
#2 0xf7c10423 in ossl_curve448_precomputed_scalarmul ()
#3 0xf7c118ed in ossl_x448_derive_public_key ()
#4 0xf7c11e8d in ossl_x448_public_from_private ()
,,,
```

failed in instruction `paddd` 

```
paddd   xmm0, XMMWORD PTR -16[eax]
```

the register `eax` is  `0xffff34c4` , the memory operand is not aligned on a 16-byte boundary,
Small case in  https://godbolt.org/z/Y8Gxdjq5f

I see the  `eax` is from `gf_sub's `  first paramenter `gf d` and can traceback to function `ossl_x448_derive_public_key's`  local varible `curve448_point_t p;` ,
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/8a82e3a7548e6a7091fbb746dae2094590c4dd5a/crypto/ec/curve448/curve448.c#L473-L480

The key is `__alignof(curve448_point_t) == 4` in x86-32，and the stack alignment is 4 bytes, so the local varible `p` is not aligned to 16 bytes which required for `paddd` instruction.
Check the https://godbolt.org/z/ra7q7qW1P for detail.

```

gcc version: 14.3
openssl: 3.5.0

---

Why  `__alignof(curve448_point_t) == 4` in x86-32?

Sorry, I not find in x86-32 spec. 
I only see https://www.sco.com/developers/devspecs/abi386-4.pdf say：

> The Intel386 architecture does not require doubleword alignment for doubleprecision values. Nevertheless, for data structure compatibility with other Intel
architectures, compilers may provide a method to align double-precision

items.title: Intermittent Freeze when running ech test

items.body:
## Summary

The `fuzz/quic-server.c` fuzzer creates a QUIC server listener but never configures a TLS certificate or private key. Since QUIC requires TLS 1.3, which mandates server-side certificates, `SSL_accept_connection()` can never complete a handshake. The entire server-side processing path (reading, writing, stream handling) is dead code.

## Bug Description

### Primary issue: Missing certificate configuration

The fuzzer sets up the QUIC server context without any certificate or private key:

```c
ctx = SSL_CTX_new(OSSL_QUIC_server_method());
SSL_CTX_set_alpn_select_cb(ctx, select_alpn, NULL);
server = SSL_new_listener(ctx, 0);
// NO SSL_CTX_use_certificate() or SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey() anywhere
```

For comparison, the regular TLS `fuzz/server.c` embeds full certificate DER arrays and calls `SSL_CTX_use_PrivateKey_ASN1()` / `SSL_CTX_use_certificate_ASN1()` for RSA, ECDSA, and DSA keys before accepting connections.

Without a certificate, the TLS 1.3 handshake cannot complete. `SSL_accept_connection()` always returns NULL, and the fuzzer stays in the `HANDSHAKING` state forever.

### Secondary issues (in dead code)

The code after a successful handshake also contains bugs, but they are never reached:

**Memory Leak**: The return value of `SSL_accept_connection()` is discarded:

```c
case HANDSHAKING:
    ret = SSL_accept_connection(stream, 0) != NULL;  // SSL* pointer lost
```

If the handshake ever succeeded, this would leak an SSL object on every call.

**I/O on wrong object**: After accepting, `stream` still points to the listener:

```c
allstreams[0] = stream = server;  // stream = listener
...
// After HANDSHAKING, stream is NOT updated
case READING:
    ret = SSL_read(stream, ...);  // reads from listener, not connection
case WRITING:
    ret = SSL_write(stream, ...); // writes to listener, not connection
```

All I/O would be performed on the listener object instead of the accepted connection.

## Evidence

### Coverage is completely flat

Running the fuzzer for 60 seconds with ASan via OSS-Fuzz:

```
#1113:  cov: 2446 ft: 2691 corp: 8   (initial)
#38511: cov: 2447 ft: 2692 corp: 9   (after 60s — no new coverage)
```

Coverage stays at ~2447 edges the entire run. The fuzzer only covers initialization, input parsing, and `SSL_accept_connection` returning NULL. No QUIC server processing code is ever reached.

### Seed corpus is trivial

The seed corpus contains only 5 files of 0-8 bytes each — consistent with a non-functional fuzzer that cannot generate meaningful test cases.

## Suggested Fix

1. Embed a test certificate and private key (similar to `fuzz/server.c`)
2. Store the accepted connection from `SSL_accept_connection()` and free it on cleanup
3. Perform I/O on the accepted connection/streams, not the listener

# Summary of the bug

A memory leak exists in the `ossl_rsa_fromdata` function in `openssl/crypto/rsa/rsa_backend.c`.

When `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_RSA_DERIVE_FROM_PQ` is set to 1, the function allocates
BIGNUM variables `p` and `q` via `OSSL_PARAM_get_BN` at lines 109-110:

```c
if ((param_p == NULL || !OSSL_PARAM_get_BN(param_p, &p))
    || (param_q == NULL || !OSSL_PARAM_get_BN(param_q, &q))) {
```

However, when the function takes an error path via `goto err` (lines 246-254),
these allocations are never freed. The error path is missing:
- `BN_clear_free(p);`  
- `BN_clear_free(q);`

## How to Trigger

1. Call `EVP_PKEY_fromdata()` with RSA parameters including:
   - Valid `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_RSA_N` (n)
   - Valid `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_RSA_E` (e) 
   - Valid `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_RSA_D` (d)
   - `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_RSA_FACTOR1` (p) - larger than n
   - `OSSL_PKEY_PARAM_RSA_FACTOR2` (q) - larger than n

2. The factor check at line 232-235 (`ossl_rsa_check_factors`) will fail because
   p and q are larger than n, triggering the error path.

3. The error path leaks p and q allocations from lines 109-110.

This is a spin-off from #28372 as requested by https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/28372#discussion_r2663668068.

In case the input certificate has already been processed by `ossl_x509v3_cache_extensions()`,
there is no need to first acquire a read lock before checking if `EXFLAG_SET` is set.
So, the very first thing the function should do is simply and very efficiently:
```
    if ((const_x->ex_flags & EXFLAG_SET) != 0) /* Cert has already been processed */
        return (const_x->ex_flags & EXFLAG_INVALID) == 0;
```

items.body:
OpenSSL 3.5 and 3.6 used to be able to do CMS signing with signed attributes using ED448.

This is broken in master by the support for hashless signature schemes for the no-attributes signing case in https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/28923/changes#diff-6f6d1c177920b274cd0a7f55d9fe98b6193c596967ed7ce7d9eb72ae4c5c0f3a

Steps to reproduce:
```
openssl req -x509 -newkey ed448 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem -nodes -subj "/CN=blah" -addext keyUsage=nonRepudiation,digitalSignature,keyEncipherment
echo "text to sign" > text.txt

# Works with OpenSSL 3.5, gives the following error with master:
#   Error adding SignerInfo with key from key.pem
openssl cms -sign -signer cert.pem -inkey key.pem -in text.txt -out out.cms -md shake256

openssl cms -verify -in out.cms -CAfile cert.pem -content text.txt
```

Note that in the example above, the `cms -sign` command is setting a digest with `-md shake256`.

RFC 8419 says the digest MUST be id-shake256-len (param: 512), however OpenSSL doesn't support this OID.  Per the example above, a user could previously set the digest to any supported algorithm.  Now they cannot.

The problem is in the code below, where the first 1 prevents the user from setting their own digest algorithm.
```
    /* RFC 8419 3.1 ED448: id-shake256-len MUST be used => NID_undef for now */
    { "ED448",              NID_undef,    1, NID_shake256, 1, },
```

So, what should happen here?  It it okay that the MUST is now being enforced where previously it wasn't?  Since id-shake256-len is not implemented, this means that ED448 can't be used with signed attributes in CMS.  Or should OpenSSL ignore the MUST (until id-shake256-len is supported?) and allow the user to override the digest despite RFC 8419?

I have a test case in 80-test_cms.t with a fix for this (change the 1 to a 0), but am looking for guidance on the second best way to proceed (the best way is probably to support id-shake256-len, but I don't have a fix for that).

This is a spin off from #28372; see also https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/28372#discussion_r2663691535

The ancient undocumented function `X509_trusted` does not at all determine whether its input cert is trusted,
but checks whether the `aux` field is non-NULL,
which is the case after successfully calling `X509_alias_set1()`, `X509_keyid_set1()`, `X509_add1_trust_object()`, and `X509_add1_reject_object()`.
So its name is pretty wrong and deceptive.

Moreover, it is of no real practical use. 
Currently it is only used in `X509_aux_print()`, where it could be replaced by `x->aux != NULL`.
So I suggest deprecating the function and replacing its mentioned trivial internal use.

items.body:
## Description
A Use-After-Free (UAF) vulnerability exists in the QUIC implementation of OpenSSL 4.0.0-dev. When a new QUIC connection is being accepted in `ossl_quic_accept_int`, a failure in `SSL_up_ref` leads to the premature destruction of the listener's shared mutex.

### Technical Details
In `ssl/quic/quic_impl.c`, the function `ossl_quic_accept_int` handles incoming QUIC connections. 

1. The function calls `create_qc_from_incoming_conn`, which initializes a proposed connection object (`qc`).
2. Crucially, `create_qc_from_incoming_conn` assigns the listener's mutex to the new connection: `qc->mutex = ql->mutex`.
3. At line 4802, `qc->pending` is set to 0.
4. At line 4803, `SSL_up_ref(&ctx.ql->obj.ssl)` is called to increment the reference count on the listener.
5. If `SSL_up_ref` fails (which can happen due to reference count overflow if the listener has ~2^31 active references, or internal allocation failures), the code enters the error block:
   ```c
   if (!SSL_up_ref(&ctx.ql->obj.ssl)) {
       SSL_free(conn_ssl);
   }
   ```
6. `SSL_free(conn_ssl)` triggers the cleanup of the connection object. Because `qc->listener` has not yet been set (it happens at line 4813), `qc_cleanup` (called during `SSL_free`) executes:
   ```c
   // ssl/quic/quic_impl.c:720
   ossl_crypto_mutex_free(&qc->mutex);
   ```
7. Since `qc->mutex` is the same pointer as `ql->mutex`, this call destroys the listener's mutex while the listener is still active and, importantly, while the current thread holds a lock on it.
8. When `ossl_quic_accept_int` reaches line 4817, it calls `qctx_unlock(&ctx)`, which attempts to unlock the now-freed mutex, resulting in a Use-After-Free and likely a crash or undefined behavior.

## OpenSSL Version
Tested on OpenSSL 4.0.0-dev (source audit).

## Steps to Reproduce
This is a logic flaw reachable via the source path `ssl/quic/quic_impl.c:4803`. While difficult to trigger in a standard environment due to the high reference count required for `SSL_up_ref` to fail, it represents a critical failure path that violates the integrity of the listener object.

A mock-based test or a debugger can trigger this by forcing `SSL_up_ref` to return 0 at the specified line.

## Potential Fix
The mutex assignment should be decoupled from the listener's lifecycle until the connection is fully validated, or the error path in `ossl_quic_accept_int` should prevent `qc_cleanup` from destroying the shared mutex if the connection initialization is aborted.

```c
// Suggested change: Set the listener back-pointer BEFORE SSL_up_ref 
// and ensure qc_cleanup respects the shared nature of the mutex.
```
items.title: [QUIC] Use-after-free on listener mutex in ossl_quic_accept_int after SSL_up_ref failure

# Summary of the bug

Race condition in `get_cert_by_subject_ex()` (`crypto/x509/by_dir.c`, lines 354-364, 415-418) and `ossl_x509_store_ctx_get_by_subject()` (`crypto/x509/x509_lu.c`). The code obtains a pointer `tmp` to an `X509_OBJECT` from the store's internal object collection while holding a read lock, releases the lock, then uses the pointer. A concurrent `X509_STORE_free()` can free the underlying memory between lock release and pointer dereference, causing use-after-free.

Both functions follow this vulnerable pattern:

```
1. Acquire read lock
2. Get pointer `tmp` from store's internal object collection
3. Release read lock
4. Use `tmp` without synchronization ← RACE WINDOW
```

Between the lock release and pointer use, another thread could free the store
and its objects, causing use-after-free.

Vulnerable pattern in `by_dir.c`:
```c
if (ossl_x509_store_read_lock(xl->store_ctx) == 0)   // Line 354: Lock
    goto finish;
// ...
tmp = sk_X509_OBJECT_value(objs, j);                  // Line 362: Get pointer
X509_STORE_unlock(xl->store_ctx);                     // Line 364: Unlock
// ...
ret->type = tmp->type;                                // Line 417: UAF
memcpy(&ret->data, &tmp->data, sizeof(ret->data));   // Line 418: UAF
```
In `x509_lu.c`:
```c
if (!ossl_x509_store_read_lock(store))  // Line 452: Lock
    return 0;
...
tmp = X509_OBJECT_retrieve_by_subject(objs, type, name);  // Get tmp pointer
...
X509_STORE_unlock(store);  // Line 461: Unlock

// RACE WINDOW - tmp points to potentially freed memory

if (ret != NULL) {
    if (!X509_OBJECT_up_ref_count(tmp))  // Line 482: USE AFTER FREE
        return -1;
    ret->type = tmp->type;  // Line 484: USE AFTER FREE
    ret->data = tmp->data;  // Line 485: USE AFTER FREE
}
```

We tested it on commit `9aeb35372fd0d988b257e2e633754ea5df83d353` from git master branch.

# Steps to reproduce

1. Build OpenSSL with ThreadSanitizer:

```bash
./Configure linux-x86_64 -fsanitize=thread -g -O1 -fno-omit-frame-pointer
make -j$(nproc)
```
2. Build and run the PoC:
 ```bash
gcc -fsanitize=thread -g -O1 -fno-omit-frame-pointer \
    -I<openssl_path>/include -L<openssl_path> \
    poc.c -o poc -Wl,-rpath,<openssl_path> \
    -lssl -lcrypto -lpthread -ldl
mkdir -p /tmp/poc_certs
./poc
 ```
# Suggested fix

- In `ossl_x509_store_ctx_get_by_subject()` and `get_cert_by_subject_ex()`,
avoid the pattern `get tmp -> unlock -> use tmp`.
- Keep the lock until `tmp` is safely handled (e.g.,
`X509_OBJECT_up_ref_count(tmp)` + copy result fields), then unlock.
- Also pin `X509_STORE` lifetime during lookup with `X509_STORE_up_ref()` /
balanced `X509_STORE_free()` on function exit to prevent concurrent store
teardown.

items.body:
In NIST SP 800-108 Rev. 1 (upd1), Section 4.1 (Counter Mode) notes that when using CMAC, a party who knows the input key KIN and can freely choose sufficient portions of:

  Label || 0x00 || Context || [L]2

may be able to control one or more derived blocks K(i). NIST provides an illustrative example in Appendix B (“Example of CMAC Key Control Security Issue”).

NIST suggests one mitigation (“one way to guard”) for CMAC in counter mode: augment the fixed input data with an additional PRF output:

  K(0) = PRF(KIN, Label || 0x00 || Context || [L]2)

and then derive:

  K(i) = PRF(KIN, [i]2 || Label || 0x00 || Context || [L]2 || K(0)), for i = 1..n.

In OpenSSL’s KBKDF [implementation](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/providers/implementations/kdfs/kbkdf.c#L218) (providers/implementations/kdfs/kbkdf.c, function derive()), counter mode currently follows the base counter-mode construction and does not incorporate the above K(0) strengthening for CMAC (K(0) is only used as the IV/K(0) value in feedback mode).

We should add K0 to PRF mitigate this issue.
items.title: OpenSSL KBKDF (CMAC counter mode): SP 800-108r1 key-control mitigation (K(0)) not implemented

items.body:
(reporting here after correspondence with openssl-security@openssl.org, since the team will not be handling this as a security issue given the malicious/compromised CA pre-req)

### Summary
OpenSSL can enforce DNS nameConstraints on CN fallback for plain CN values but skip enforcement for wildcard-containing CN values, while hostname matching still accepts those wildcard CNs by default. A constrained intermediate CA can therefore issue no-SAN leaf certificates that validate for out-of-scope hostnames.

### Details
The documentation says DNS constraints apply to subject CN when CN fallback is used (`doc/man3/X509_VERIFY_PARAM_set_flags.pod`). Hostname matching defaults allow partial wildcards (`doc/man3/X509_check_host.pod`). In code, `NAME_CONSTRAINTS_check_CN()` calls `cn2dnsid()` and skips constraint matching when `idlen == 0;` thus, wildcard-containing CN values get filtered out (`crypto/x509/v3_ncons.c`). Hostname verification later accepts those wildcard CN patterns (`crypto/x509/v3_utl.c`), creating a mismatch between constraint enforcement and hostname acceptance.

In short, any CN value that OpenSSL will accept as a DNS reference identifier for hostname verification should be subject to the same DNS nameConstraints checks when CN fallback is in effect, but is not.

I've repro'd the findings on 3.6.1 and master commit e003d87f8abdee57e8f26de7d89358b808b73989.

### PoC
Run the attached below; expected output:

```bash
CN=www.evil.com
error 47 at 0 depth lookup: permitted subtree violation
error leaf.pem: verification failed
leaf.pem: OK
leaf.pem: OK
CN=san-control
error 47 at 0 depth lookup: permitted subtree violation
error leaf.pem: verification failed
Intermediate constrains to only DNS:example.com
Baseline return code (should fail validating CN=www.evil.com): 2      # subtree violation above
Bypass return code (should succeed with CN=w*.evil.com): 0
Bypass return code (should succeed with CN=*.evil.com): 0
SAN baseline return code (should fail with SAN DNS:*.evil.com): 2.   # subtree violation above
```

<details>
<summary>Script (Click to expand)</summary>

```bash

set -euo pipefail

OPENSSL="/opt/openssl-3.6.1/bin/openssl"
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH="/opt/openssl-3.6.1/lib64"
HOSTNAME="www.evil.com"

issue_chain() {
  local dir="$1"
  local cn="$2"
  local san_dns="${3:-}"

  mkdir -p "$dir"
  cd "$dir"

  cat > openssl.cnf <<'EOC'
[ req ]
distinguished_name = dn
prompt = no
[ dn ]
CN = dummy
EOC

  cat > root_ext.cnf <<'EOC'
[ v3_root ]
basicConstraints = critical, CA:true
keyUsage = critical, keyCertSign, cRLSign
subjectKeyIdentifier = hash
EOC

  cat > int_ext.cnf <<'EOC'
[ v3_ca ]
basicConstraints = critical, CA:true, pathlen:0
keyUsage = critical, keyCertSign, cRLSign
subjectKeyIdentifier = hash
authorityKeyIdentifier = keyid:always,issuer
nameConstraints = critical, permitted;DNS:example.com
EOC

  cat > leaf_ext.cnf <<'EOC'
[ v3_leaf ]
basicConstraints = critical, CA:false
keyUsage = critical, digitalSignature, keyEncipherment
extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth
EOC

  if [[ -n "$san_dns" ]]; then
    printf 'subjectAltName = DNS:%s\n' "$san_dns" >> leaf_ext.cnf
  fi

  export OPENSSL_CONF="$dir/openssl.cnf"

  "$OPENSSL" genrsa -out root.key 2048 >/dev/null 2>&1
  "$OPENSSL" req -new -config openssl.cnf -key root.key -subj '/CN=Root' -out root.csr >/dev/null 2>&1
  "$OPENSSL" x509 -req -in root.csr -signkey root.key -out root.pem -days 365 -extfile root_ext.cnf -extensions v3_root >/dev/null 2>&1

  "$OPENSSL" genrsa -out int.key 2048 >/dev/null 2>&1
  "$OPENSSL" req -new -config openssl.cnf -key int.key -subj '/CN=Constrained-Intermediate' -out int.csr >/dev/null 2>&1
  "$OPENSSL" x509 -req -in int.csr -CA root.pem -CAkey root.key -CAcreateserial -out int.pem -days 365 -extfile int_ext.cnf -extensions v3_ca >/dev/null 2>&1

  "$OPENSSL" genrsa -out leaf.key 2048 >/dev/null 2>&1
  "$OPENSSL" req -new -config openssl.cnf -key leaf.key -subj "/CN=${cn}" -out leaf.csr >/dev/null 2>&1

  set +e

  return "$rc"

workdir="$(mktemp -d /tmp/vh-openssl-f001-sh.XXXXXX)"
trap 'rm -rf "$workdir"' EXIT

if issue_chain "$workdir/baseline" "www.evil.com"; then

else

fi

if issue_chain "$workdir/bypass_partial" "w*.evil.com"; then

else

fi

fi

else

fi

echo "Baseline return code (should fail validating CN=www.evil.com): ${baseline_rc}"
echo "Bypass return code (should succeed with CN=w*.evil.com): ${bypass_partial_rc}"
echo "Bypass return code (should succeed with CN=*.evil.com): ${bypass_full_rc}"
echo "SAN baseline return code (should fail with SAN DNS:*.evil.com): ${san_control_rc}"

if [[ "$baseline_rc" -eq 0 ]]; then
  echo "unexpected baseline success"
  exit 1
fi

if [[ "$bypass_partial_rc" -ne 0 ]]; then
  echo "partial wildcard bypass did not reproduce"
  exit 1
fi

A malicious/compromised constrained intermediate CA can bypass delegated DNS namespace constraints and impersonate out-of-scope TLS endpoints without verifier-local privileged changes.

items.body:
## Issue found: Client Authentication Is Fail-Open by Default

Weakness classification:

- CWE-295: Improper Certificate Validation
- CWE-297: Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch
## Reviewed Version

- Repository: local clone of `openssl/openssl`
- Branch reviewed: `master`
- Commit reviewed: `5aaf9746f6`
- Version data in tree: `4.0.0-dev`

### In Short

An OpenSSL client starts in a state where certificate verification is not fatal,
 and hostname verification is a separate opt-in API. If a downstream
 application does not explicitly enable both, a MITM can present a wrong or
 otherwise invalid certificate and still get a successful handshake.

### Why this matters

This is a deployment risk.

In practice, many clients:

- set SNI but do not enable hostname verification
- assume certificate verification is fatal by default
- or partially configure verification and think they are covered

That creates a straightforward attack path for active network attackers.

### Attack conditions

- the target uses OpenSSL as a TLS client
- the application does not correctly enable:
  - fatal certificate verification
  - hostname verification
- the attacker can intercept and modify traffic

### Impact

- MITM against TLS clients
- credential theft
- request or response tampering
- service impersonation

### Code references

- `openssl/ssl/ssl_lib.c:4072`
  - default context setup uses `ret->verify_mode = SSL_VERIFY_NONE`
- `openssl/ssl/statem/statem_clnt.c:2121-2128`
  - certificate verification is performed, but handshake failure only happens
    when `s->verify_mode != SSL_VERIFY_NONE`
- `openssl/include/openssl/ssl.h.in:1839-1842`
  - hostname verification is exposed as separate APIs:
    `SSL_set1_host()` and `SSL_set_hostflags()`

### Suggested fix

Any of the following would reduce risk:

- make fatal certificate verification the safer default for clients
- provide a clearly documented "safe client profile" API that enables both:
  - fatal certificate verification
  - hostname verification
- if compatibility blocks a default change, strengthen the official guidance so
  insecure client setup is harder to miss

Please let us know how you think about these issues. Thank you for the work on Openssl!
items.title: Security hardening issue:client authentication is fail-open by default

items.body:
## Issue Found: TLS Record Compression Still Exists as Opt-In Functionality

Weakness classification:

- CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
- CWE-16: Configuration

### In Short

OpenSSL disables TLS record compression by default, which is the right default.
 However, the code to register and negotiate TLS compression is still present.
 If a deployment re-enables it, old compression side channels such as
 CRIME-class attacks can come back.

### Why this matters

This is not a default-path vulnerability. It is a legacy attack surface that
 can still be reintroduced by configuration, compatibility layers, or forks.

### Attack conditions

- TLS compression is re-enabled
- the attacker can influence plaintext near secrets
- the attacker can observe ciphertext length or a related oracle

### Impact

- leakage of cookies, bearer tokens, or similar secrets in affected traffic

### Code references

- `openssl/ssl/ssl_lib.c:4234`
  - default options include `SSL_OP_NO_COMPRESSION`
- `openssl/ssl/ssl_ciph.c:2021-2059`
  - compression methods can still be registered with
    `SSL_COMP_add_compression_method()`
- `openssl/ssl/statem/statem_srvr.c:2089-2101`
  - server-side compression negotiation logic is still present when compression
    is allowed and methods are configured

### Suggested fix

- ideally remove TLS record compression support entirely
- if that is not practical, keep it clearly documented as unsafe legacy
  functionality

items.body:
## Problem:

Currently, OpenSSL (except in FIPS mode, ref: #19989) does not enforce EMS negotiation for compatibility reasons.

This means that when establishing a connection with a peer that does not support EMS, if session resumption and renegotiation are enabled, users are vulnerable to triple handshake attacks.


## Proposed Solution

Change OpenSSL's behavior such that during session resumption, if the old session was negotiated without EMS, refuse to resume that session—instead require a new full handshake. This way, connections with peers that do not support EMS remain possible (for compatibility), but session resumption will not introduce triple handshake attack risk.

This improves security by preventing vulnerable session reuse without breaking compatibility with non-EMS peers.
items.title: Enforce EMS during Session Resumption to prevent Triple Handshake Attacks

items.title: Incorrect error return in ppc_aes_gcm_cipher_update

items.body:
https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/providers/implementations/ciphers/cipher_aes_gcm_hw_ppc.inc#L44-L71

In `ppc_aes_gcm_crypt()`, the assembly functions `ppc_aes_gcm_encrypt`/`ppc_aes_gcm_decrypt` are declared as returning `size_t` (64-bit on PPC64) in `include/crypto/aes_platform.h:85-90`. However, their return values are stored in `int s` (line 47) and accumulated in `int ndone` (line 48), both 32-bit signed.

```c
int s = 0;        // 32-bit signed — should be size_t
int ndone = 0;    // 32-bit signed — should be size_t
// ...
s = encrypt ? ppc_aes_gcm_encrypt(in, out, len, key, ivec, Xi)   // returns size_t
            : ppc_aes_gcm_decrypt(in, out, len, key, ivec, Xi);  // returns size_t
ndone += s;
return ndone;     // implicit int → size_t conversion
```

While the primary EVP entrypoints take length as an `int` (and therefore can't overflow this), `EVP_Cipher()` takes length as an `unsigned int` and therefore can.
items.title: ppc_aes_gcm_crypt: integer truncation for inputs >2GB

The implementation of BIO_vsnprintf() changed from using a self-cooked _dopr() which is using fmtstr() for string argument formatting (3.6) to the platform vsnprintf() (4.x).

and the NULL argument comes from crypto/conf/conf_mod.c:

```
 75 static int conf_diagnostics(const CONF *cnf)

items.body:
<!--
Thank you for your bug report. If this is your first one,
please take the time to read the following lines before posting it.

NOTE:

    If you're asking about how to use OpenSSL, this isn't the right
    forum.  Please see our User Support resources:
    https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/SUPPORT.md

Please remember to tell us in what OpenSSL version you found the issue.

For build issues:

    If this is a build issue, please include the configuration output
    as well as a log of all errors.  Don't forget to include the exact
    commands you typed.

    With OpenSSL before 1.1.1, the configuration output comes from the
    configuration command.  With OpenSSL 1.1.1 and on, it's the output
    of `perl configdata.pm --dump`

For other issues:

    If it isn't a build issue, example code or commands to reproduce
    the issue is highly appreciated.
    Also, please remember to tell us if you worked with your own
    OpenSSL build or if it is system provided.

Please remember to put ``` lines before and after any commands plus
output and code, like this:

    ```
    $ echo output output output
    output output output
    ```

    ```
    #include <stdio.h>

    int main() {
        int foo = 1;
        printf("%d\n", foo);
    }
    ```
-->
It was triggered accidentally on my machine, but after switching to a different machine, the issue disappeared; ultimately, it turned out to be an implementation problem in crypto/sm4/asm/vpsm4_ex-armv8.pl
@https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/f0de2ebb561521ffebd1d54899b8dfbbbc750cd2/crypto/sm4/asm/vpsm4_ex-armv8.pl#L478-L484

When .Lsbox_magic is near the end of a page, symbol + offset can cross into the next page.
 For example,  `.Lsbox_magic`'s address is 0xaaaaaaa7ff0, and `.Lsbox_magic`'s low 12-bits = 0xff0, offset = 80 (0x50), so 0xff0 + 0x50 = 0x1040. 
And the address of `.Lsbox_magic+80` will be 0xaaaaaaa7040, not the actually 0xaaaaaaa8040. And the remaining calculations will all result in errors.
